author: maurelian
content:
Hey <@792404665068158998> thanks for the details here. I just wanted to respond to this quickly: 
> Contracts can usually be designed modularly and split across multiple transactions.
For context, our largest transaction is when we deploy a new L2 system, which requires something like 10 new proxies on the L1, and it costs roughly 11MM gas pre-amsterdam. Post-amsterdam is TBD of course, and we will test as soon as we have an EVM we can run against. 

We recently invested a good amount of effort to reduce the cost of deploying a new system by ensuring all contracts are proxied instead of requiring a full blown implementation deployment, which got us more safety margin against the Fusaka limit. So yes, if Amsterdam repricing brings us to the Fusaka limit again then we will have to break the deployent process up into smaller chunks, but it would be frustrating if we find ourselves on a treadmill of reacting to shifting limits and gas prices. Do you anticipate more repricing in future hard forks?

author: nero_eth
content:
It’s very likely that repricings will continue to happen in the future. As the protocol evolves and we learn more about new bottlenecks, adjusting gas costs is one of the main tools we have to keep the system balanced while scalable.
Because of that, it’s generally safest to design contracts (and contract deployments) such that individual transactions don’t rely on exceeding the ~16.78M gas limit (or even getting to close). In practice this usually means structuring deployments or operations so they can be split across multiple transactions if needed. Ofc, the 16.78M limit could also be raised at some point in the future too if it became too much of a constrain for apps.
E.g. the 10 newly deployed proxies, do they need an atomic deployment or could they technically be deployed independently (even if this would mean one would need to stich them together afterwards)?

author: maurelian
content: they can technically be deployed independently, though it would be an onerous refactoring.

author: nero_eth
content:
For cases like this, there should be sufficient lead time. Repricing changes are not introduced without giving the ecosystem time to adapt. In practice, teams can usually keep things as they are, track upcoming forks, and only refactor if an issue actually appears on a testnet ahead of the fork.
And feedback like yours is valuable and absolutely taken into account when considering future repricings.
